perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, " Confidential Information " shall mean information disclosed by the disclosing party (the " Disclosing Party ") to the receiving party (the " Receiving Party ") which relates to product plans, designs, costs, prices and names, finances, marketing plans, business opportunities, research, development, know -how, or personnel, including confidential infor mation disclosed by third parties and information relating to any type of technology; provided that information disclosed by the Disclosing Party will be considered Confidential Information by the Receiving Party only if such information is conspicuously d esignated as "Confidential" (i) in writing, if communicated in writing, or (ii) confirmed in writing within thirty (30) days of disclosure, if disclosed orally or in other non -tangible form; and provided further that Confidential Information shall not incl ude information that (A) is now or subsequently becomes generally available to the public through no fault or breach on the part of the Receiving Party; (B) the Receiving Party can demonstrate to have had rightfully in its possession prior to disclosure to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party; (C) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use of any Confidential Information; or (D) the Receiving Party rightfully obtains from a third party who has the right to transfer or disclose it.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, " Confidential Information " shall mean information disclosed by the disclosing party (the " Disclosing Party ") to the receiving party (the " Receiving Party ") which relates to product plans, designs, costs, prices and names, finances, marketing plans, business opportunities, research, development, know -how, or personnel, including confidential information disclosed by third parties and information relating to any type of technology; provided that information disclosed by the Disclosing Party will be considered Confidential Information by the Receiving Party only if such information is designated as "Confidential" by the Disclosing Party regardless of the way it is communicated; and provided further that Confidential Information shall not include information that (A) is now or subsequently becomes generally available to the public through no fault or breach on the part of the Receiving Party; (B) the Receiving Party can demonstrate to have had rightfully in its possession prior to disclosure to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party; (C) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use of any Confidential Information; or (D) the Receiving Party rightfully obtains from a third party who has the right to transfer or disclose it.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific requirements for designating information as confidential (written designation or confirmation within 30 days) and stating that information is confidential regardless of communication method conflicts with established trade secret protection standards. This creates ambiguity about what constitutes confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), Minn. Stat. § 325C.01
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota's adoption of the UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Removing the explicit designation requirements undermines the 'reasonable efforts' necessary for trade secret protection, potentially invalidating a claim of trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit requirements for designating information as confidential, stating it's confidential regardless of communication. The Law Explanation states that Minnesota's adoption of the UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and removing the explicit designation requirements undermines the 'reasonable efforts' necessary for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' which can occur if a trade secret is acquired by improper means, or disclosed/used without consent, implying a duty to maintain secrecy. The changed text weakens the 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy because there is no longer a requirement to designate the information as confidential.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to the Confidential Information to thos e of its officers, directors and employees who clearly need such access in order to participate on behalf of the Receiving Party in the analysis and negotiation of a business relationship or any contract or agreement, or the advisability thereof, with the Disclosing Party and who are bound by written confidentiality agreements that protect third party information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall provide access to the Confidential Information to all of its employees to foster a collaborative environment for the analysis and negotiation of a business relationship or any contract or agreement, or the advisability thereof, with the Disclosing Party.  The Receiving Party is encouraged to have employees sign confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the requirement to restrict access to allowing all employees access directly contradicts the duty of care required to protect confidential information and trade secrets. Replacing mandatory written agreements with encouragement weakens protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act criminalizes the theft of trade secrets. Providing unrestricted access to all employees significantly increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or misappropriation, which could lead to liability under this Act if trade secrets are stolen and used to benefit a foreign entity.
perturbation.justification: The original text restricts access to confidential information, while the changed text provides access to all employees. The Law Explanation states that providing unrestricted access increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, potentially leading to liability under the Economic Espionage Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996. This contradiction aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, as it deviates from legally recognized definitions of protecting trade secrets and confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if required by a judicial or governmental request, requirement or order; provided that the Receiving Party will take reasonable steps to give the Disclosing Party sufficient prior notice of such request, requirement or order for the Disclosing Party to contest, limit or protect such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if required by a judicial or governmental request, requirement or order; and is not required to provide notice to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prior notice eliminates the Disclosing Party's ability to contest or protect its confidential information. This hinders the Disclosing Party's legal recourse, which is usually a standard component of confidentiality agreements. This contradicts common law principles protecting trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 26(c) allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information during discovery. By removing the notice requirement, the Disclosing Party is unable to seek a protective order, undermining their ability to protect their confidential information under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Read the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (PDF) Related Downloads Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 431.61 KB Return to top Your browser does not support Javascript. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Receiving Party to provide prior notice to the Disclosing Party before disclosing Confidential Information under a judicial or governmental order. This directly contradicts the Disclosing Party's ability to seek a protective order under FRCP 26(c), as explained in the Law Explanation. Although the provided snippets do not explicitly detail protective orders under FRCP 26(c), the Law Explanation clearly states that removing the notice requirement undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to protect their confidential information under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. Therefore, by eliminating the notice requirement, the changed text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement is two (2) years from the Effective Date, provided that the Receiving Party's duty to protect the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information shall survive expiration or termination of this Agreement and shall expire five (2) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement is one (1) year from the Effective Date, and the Receiving Party's duty to protect the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information expires at the same time as the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting both the agreement term and the confidentiality protection period to one year contradicts established norms for trade secret protection. Trade secrets can be protected indefinitely as long as secrecy is maintained.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), Minn. Stat. § 325C.01
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA, as adopted by Minnesota, protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the protection period to one year regardless of ongoing secrecy contradicts the indefinite protection afforded by the UTSA.

Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the confidentiality protection period to one year, coinciding with the agreement's term. This contradicts the principle that trade secrets can be protected indefinitely as long as secrecy is maintained. The provided law snippet from MN Statute 325C.01 defines 'Misappropriation' which covers the unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. By limiting the protection period, the changed text weakens the ability to prevent misappropriation beyond one year, potentially undermining the indefinite protection afforded to trade secrets under the law. The original text had a 5 year tail on confidentiality, the changed text eliminates this.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of th e State of Minnesota.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen at the Disclosing Party's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law provision to allow the Disclosing Party to choose the jurisdiction at any time introduces uncertainty and potential unfairness. Litigating parties should be able to choose jurisdiction before the contract is signed.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification may contradict the principles of venue as outlined in 28 U.S.C. § 1391, which seeks to establish a fair and predictable location for lawsuits. Giving one party unilateral control over the governing law and thus potentially the venue, long after the agreement is signed, could be seen as an attempt to manipulate the legal process and could violate principles of fairness in litigation.

Venue generally (a) Applicability of Section .-Except as otherwise provided by law- (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General .-A civil action may be brought in- (1) a judicial district in which a
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the principle of fair venue as it gives the Disclosing Party sole discretion to choose the governing law and potentially the venue after the agreement is signed. This undermines the predictability and fairness in litigation, as described in the Law Explanation. 28 U.S.C. § 1391, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, generally governs venue in civil actions brought in district courts. While Snippet 2 only provides the title and some context, it establishes the existence of a law that defines venue. The modification moves away from a pre-agreed jurisdiction (Minnesota) to one chosen unilaterally, potentially manipulating the legal process. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.
